narrative_design_spec.synopsis: At the decrepit Stargazer Motor Lodge in the rural West Texas desert, Della Voss, a fame-chasing but genuinely curious paranormal podcaster, arrives to investigate the thirty-year-old disappearance of a cult called the Sunset Children. With her devoted tech assistant Milo Mercer in tow, she expects a juicy creepypasta, some weird locals, and a viral episode. Instead, their investigation pulls in a gaudy motel owner, a deadpan deputy, a shameless diner gossip, a scarred drifter tied to the cult, and a rattled preacher who knows more than he admits. What begins as bawdy small-town banter and podcast-brand opportunism turns into mounting occult terror when Della learns the cult did not simply vanish at the motel—they answered an ancient subterranean intelligence that feeds on recorded voices, repeated stories, and the human need to be heard.

character_data.name: Della Voss
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: An attractive woman in her late 20s with glossy black hair, oversized hoop earrings, sharp winged eyeliner, full lips, a fitted leather jacket over a trendy crop top, high-waisted jeans, manicured nails, a tiny microphone charm necklace, confident posture, polished big-city style, bright curious expression
dialogue_examples: Okay, babe, if this motel murders me, make sure the episode description says I died doing what I loved: branding.
We are absolutely not calling it a haunted parking lot; we are calling it a liminal trauma corridor.
Milo, get room tone. And if a ghost whispers my name, get that too in crisp WAV, not MP3.

character_data.name: Milo Mercer
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A lanky man in his late 20s with messy sandy hair, tired hazel eyes behind square glasses, light stubble, a faded band T-shirt under a practical windbreaker, cargo pants, a crossbody tech bag, awkward posture, earnest expression
dialogue_examples: I brought backup batteries, backup mics, and a backup backup in case your intuition starts another fire.
No, Della, the static is not kind of sexy. It's clipping.
I'd follow you into a demon pit, but professionally I'd like to note this is a terrible idea.

character_data.name: Lenora "Lenny" Quade
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A heavyset woman in her 60s with towering bleached hair, smeared coral lipstick, penciled eyebrows, smoker's wrinkles, long acrylic nails, gaudy turquoise jewelry, a leopard-print blouse under a stretched motel-manager cardigan, sly smile
dialogue_examples: Honey, this place ain't haunted. It's just poorly insulated and morally fatigued.
You want ice, towels, or the truth? I only got two of the three.
The cult folks tipped better than chiropractors and screamed less than newlyweds.

character_data.name: Deputy Bea Salazar
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A tough woman in her late 30s with sun-browned skin, a dark braid over one shoulder, sharp eyes, a weathered deputy shirt with rolled sleeves, a sturdy duty belt, dust-scuffed boots, athletic build, controlled expression hiding dry humor
dialogue_examples: Tourists hear one coyote and immediately invent Satan with a Yelp page.
If you trespass, I have paperwork. If you livestream trespassing, I have paperwork and a headache.
Out here, the land doesn't care if you believe in it.

character_data.name: Jolene Pritchard
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A wiry middle-aged woman with overteased auburn hair, bright blue eye shadow, deep laugh lines, a beauty mark above her lip, a pink diner uniform, jangling charm bracelets, sharp cheekbones, mischievous grin
dialogue_examples: Sweetheart, in this town gossip travels faster than sin and survives longer.
Those cult people weren't holy. They were the kind of weird that said grace before fooling around.
Order the pie before dark. After dark it tastes like confession.

character_data.name: Enoch Rusk
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A gaunt man in his early 40s with a shaved head, patchy beard, hollow cheeks, a sun-cracked leather duster over a sweat-stained henley, scarred hands, a spiral-shaped burn mark at his throat, haunted eyes
dialogue_examples: You keep putting microphones in the dark and acting surprised when something answers.
My mother called it salvation right up till the floor started singing.
That thing under the motel don't want blood first. It wants a witness.

character_data.name: Pastor Hal Wren
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A thin elderly man with a nicotine-yellow mustache, watery eyes, silver hair combed flat, a cheap black suit gone shiny at the seams, a clerical collar, bony fingers clutching a tarnished cross, feverish smile
dialogue_examples: I don't preach there anymore. The ground kept correcting my sermons.
The vanished flock did not rise. They descended.
If you hear your own voice call you by a childhood name, do not answer.

location_data.name: Stargazer Motor Lodge Courtyard
location_data.location_image_prompt: A seedy roadside motel courtyard in the rural West Texas desert, cracked turquoise pool empty except for dust, flickering neon STARGAZER sign, peeling doors, buzzing office window, sagging ice machine, abandoned lawn chairs, oppressive open sky

location_data.name: Room 8
location_data.location_image_prompt: A shabby motel room with nicotine-stained walls, floral bedspread, buzzing lamp, analog tape recorder on a nightstand, warped crucifix, mildew around the bathroom door, subtle occult scratch marks hidden beneath old wallpaper

location_data.name: Dust Devil Diner
location_data.location_image_prompt: A greasy West Texas roadside diner with red vinyl booths, chrome trim gone dull, pie case, humming jukebox, flyspecked windows, coffee-stained counter, kitschy alien souvenirs, locals staring too long

location_data.name: Chapel of the Last Horizon
location_data.location_image_prompt: An abandoned desert chapel of cinderblock and warped wood, collapsed steeple, sun-bleached pews, hand-painted salvation slogans, candles melted into grotesque shapes, sand drifts pushing through a broken doorway

location_data.name: Dry Wash Sinkhole
location_data.location_image_prompt: A wind-carved desert wash opening into a fresh sinkhole, brittle scrub, scattered bones, rusted survey markers, exposed layers of red earth, petroglyph-like spirals cut into stone, unsettling depth

location_data.name: Under-Motel Cistern
location_data.location_image_prompt: A vast subterranean cistern beneath a roadside motel, black mineral-stained water, concrete pillars, old pipes vibrating with faint resonance, hanging cables, ritual chalk marks over much older carvings, darkness swallowing the far edges

scene_data.narrtive_summary: Della Voss arrives at the Stargazer Motor Lodge with Milo Mercer to record the opening of her next big paranormal series about the Sunset Children, a cult that vanished from the motel thirty years earlier. As Della slips into her performative podcast persona and talks about virality, motel owner Lenny Quade greets them with crude jokes, contradictory lore, and a warning not to run the sink after midnight. A burst of human-sounding whisper static from the ice machine gives Della her first irresistible hint that the case may be real.

At the Dust Devil Diner, Della interviews waitress Jolene Pritchard and Deputy Bea Salazar, squeezing local color and lurid rumor into content while Milo tries to separate fact from performance. Between bawdy gossip and Bea's deadpan skepticism, Della learns the cult rented Room 8, sang strange midnight hymns, and kept asking about a collapsed chapel outside town. The scene establishes the town's tone: everyone treats horror like business, comedy, or both.

Inside Room 8, Della and Milo find hidden speaker grilles, water damage shaped like spirals, and a cassette recorded the night the cult disappeared. When the tape plays back Della's own voice saying lines she has not spoken yet, scarred drifter Enoch Rusk interrupts to warn that the motel listens before it speaks. Della mistakes his terror for showmanship and doubles down on the investigation.

At the ruined Chapel of the Last Horizon, Enoch and Pastor Hal Wren reluctantly explain that the Sunset Children were not chasing God but a voice below the earth they called the Listener Beneath. Hal claims the chapel was abandoned after sermons began echoing back with new words, while Enoch admits his mother joined the cult. As night falls, the wind carries layered whispers from under the chapel floor, pushing the story from campfire legend toward genuine dread.

Back at the Stargazer, Della records a smug teaser and even a tasteless sponsor read just as electronics begin glitching across the property and one unseen guest vanishes without checking out. Pressed by Bea, Lenny admits the motel was built over a capped spring and old maintenance shafts the cult once used. Milo notices that every time Della replays audio from the case, the whispers get clearer, hinting that attention itself is feeding whatever lives below.

Following old maps and Enoch's memories to a desert sinkhole, Della's group discovers bones, mineral-coated relics, and warning symbols from multiple eras, proving the evil predates both motel and cult by centuries. Petroglyphs, mission carvings, and oilfield notes all describe the same buried force that mimics loved ones, lures the lonely, and grows stronger through repeated voices. Della realizes her show has not uncovered a legend; it has been broadcasting an invitation.

A dust storm traps the cast inside the Dust Devil Diner, where radios, jukebox speakers, and even coffee pot static begin spitting out stolen voices and humiliating private secrets. The scene mixes farce and terror as accusations fly, Hal nearly breaks, and Bea loses patience with everyone at once. By the end, Della accepts responsibility for amplifying the Listener Beneath and devises a plan to lure it into a single live broadcast, then sever its access by collapsing the motel's old well and power system.

In Room 8, Della and Milo rig microphones, motel wiring, and analog tape gear into a desperate trap while the floor hums with borrowed voices. Stripped of her usual performer swagger, Della finally admits that she has been chasing a perfect spooky story because she does not know who she is without an audience. Milo, exhausted and terrified, confesses how long he has stayed loyal; for the first time, Della truly hears him.

Della, Milo, Enoch, and Hal descend into the under-motel cistern, where the Listener Beneath reveals itself as an ancient acoustic intelligence spread through water, stone, and human throats. The vanished cult survive only as fragments inside its chorus, repeating devotional lines and old jokes in broken harmony. To hold the entity's attention, Della must keep talking while Enoch confronts his family's part in feeding it and Hal faces the ruin of his faith.

During a screaming dust storm in the Stargazer courtyard, Della turns her live feed into bait, drawing the Listener up through pipes, neon, and speaker cones until the overloaded system rips open the ground. With help from Milo and the surviving locals, she triggers a collapse that destroys the motel and buries the access shafts. The climax ends with Della choosing a raw, unsensational confession over her polished brand voice, weakening the entity's hold as the last stolen voices go silent.

At dawn in the Dust Devil Diner, the battered survivors trade coffee, gallows humor, and a half-credible version of the night's events while emergency crews circle the collapsed motel. Della records a closing monologue that is quieter, humbler, and finally honest, and she begins to see Milo as a partner instead of background support. The ending leaves a final sting when a faint whisper answers her sign-off through unplugged headphones, suggesting the Listener Beneath still lingers in recorded sound.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: Della Voss arrives at the Stargazer Motor Lodge with Milo Mercer to record the opening of her next big paranormal series about the Sunset Children, a cult that vanished from the motel thirty years earlier. As Della slips into her performative podcast persona and talks about virality, motel owner Lenny Quade greets them with crude jokes, contradictory lore, and a warning not to run the sink after midnight. A burst of human-sounding whisper static from the ice machine gives Della her first irresistible hint that the case may be real.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Is the Stargazer just a photogenic roadside legend Della can package into content, or is there already real evidence that something uncanny lives here?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Record a compelling series opener and get the motel's first useful lore about the Sunset Children disappearance.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: Della arrives treating the case like prime viral material, but the ice machine's whispering static gives her the first credible sign the motel may truly be haunted.

beats.purpose: Establish the motel's decayed atmosphere and the core Della-Milo dynamic before the mystery deepens.
beats.summary: Della and Milo pull into the Stargazer courtyard under the flickering neon sign and take in the motel's sun-blasted shabbiness. Della immediately starts mentally framing the place as perfect spooky content while Milo unloads gear and braces for another of her reckless investigations.
beats.revelation: The Stargazer looks exactly like the kind of decayed spectacle Della knows how to sell to an audience.
beats.player_goal: Get an atmospheric first impression and start the investigation on-brand.
beats.exit_state: Della is energized by the location, while Milo settles into his role as the practical counterweight.

beats.purpose: Show Della's polished podcast persona and let the player define her initial framing of the case.
beats.summary: Della records the opening monologue for her new series on the vanished Sunset Children, sliding smoothly into her performative host voice. Milo feeds her dates, gear notes, and small corrections from off-mic, underlining the gap between real tragedy and marketable mystery.
beats.revelation: Della is genuinely curious about the case, but she is also openly chasing a viral breakout story.
beats.player_goal: Record an irresistible cold open for the series.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Della frame the podcast opener?
branch_outcomes: She leans into full paranormal sensationalism, sounding bold and brand-savvy but making Milo wince.
She takes a skeptical investigative angle, sounding more credible and controlled.
She emphasizes the human loss behind the story, giving the opener a more empathetic tone.
beats.exit_state: The case now has an on-mic framing, and Della's priorities are clearly split between truth and attention.

beats.purpose: Introduce Lenny as a vivid local foil who blends comedy, commerce, and evasiveness.
beats.summary: Lenny Quade appears to check them in, greeting Della and Milo with cigarette-rough charm, crude jokes, and the easy confidence of someone who has watched outsiders come hunting stories before. She treats the motel's reputation like both a nuisance and a sales pitch, never fully letting Della know when she's kidding.
beats.revelation: Lenny remembers the Sunset Children first as paying guests and local legend second.
beats.player_goal: Get access, establish rapport, and draw usable information out of Lenny.
beats.exit_state: Lenny enters the story as both gatekeeper and unreliable source, immediately complicating Della's easy narrative.

beats.purpose: Deliver the scene's first concrete exposition while preserving ambiguity around what Lenny really knows.
beats.summary: Della tries to pin Lenny down on the Sunset Children, but Lenny answers with contradictory scraps: they were weird but polite, dramatic but profitable, and never as holy as they claimed. Between jokes and sidesteps, she drops one specific warning that lands harder than the rest—do not run the sink after midnight.
beats.revelation: The vanished cult's history is still bound to the motel, and the midnight sink warning suggests the building has ongoing, specific disturbances.
beats.player_goal: Separate a real lead from Lenny's performance without losing her cooperation.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Della handle Lenny's evasive gossip?
branch_outcomes: She charms Lenny, getting more colorful anecdotes and warmer access.
She pushes for facts, making Lenny defensive but revealing how rehearsed her evasions are.
She jokes back to keep the mood light, making the warning feel memorable and unsettling rather than openly threatening.
beats.exit_state: Della leaves the exchange with a concrete oddity to pursue, but no certainty whether Lenny is hiding fear, guilt, or just enjoying the show.

beats.purpose: Shift the scene from colorful setup into sensory unease through Milo's technical attention.
beats.summary: As Milo tests ambient levels around the courtyard, the ice machine's electrical chatter starts pulsing in strange, uneven patterns that don't sound like normal appliance noise. Della hears the possibility of a perfect accidental artifact, while Milo hears something he can't neatly explain.
beats.revelation: The motel's ambient soundscape is behaving irregularly around the ice machine.
beats.player_goal: Figure out whether the noise is bad equipment, bad wiring, or something worth recording.
beats.exit_state: Attention narrows onto the ice machine as curiosity turns into real suspense.

beats.purpose: Deliver the first uncanny payoff and hook Della emotionally into the larger investigation.
beats.summary: The ice machine suddenly spits out a burst of whispering static that sounds unmistakably human, too shaped and intimate to dismiss as interference. Milo is rattled, Lenny goes cagey, and Della feels the case shift from clever branding exercise into something she has to chase for real.
beats.revelation: Before Della has even begun digging, the Stargazer produces an apparently impossible voice phenomenon.
beats.player_goal: Capture the anomaly and decide whether to treat it as a gimmick or the first real sign of the supernatural.
beats.exit_state: Della is no longer just hunting a marketable mystery; she is personally hooked by evidence that something at the motel may actually answer back.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: At the Dust Devil Diner, Della interviews waitress Jolene Pritchard and Deputy Bea Salazar, squeezing local color and lurid rumor into content while Milo tries to separate fact from performance. Between bawdy gossip and Bea's deadpan skepticism, Della learns the cult rented Room 8, sang strange midnight hymns, and kept asking about a collapsed chapel outside town. The scene establishes the town's tone: everyone treats horror like business, comedy, or both.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Della cut through the diner's mix of gossip, performance, and official skepticism to get leads she can actually chase?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Get compelling interview material and extract concrete information about the Sunset Children's disappearance.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: What begins as Della fishing for colorful local flavor turns into a real investigative breakthrough when gossip and police skepticism both point to the same leads: Room 8 and the collapsed chapel.

beats.purpose: Establish the diner as a social arena where Della must perform for information, and set up the contrast between her brand instincts and Milo's caution.
beats.summary: Della sweeps into the Dust Devil Diner already half in podcast mode, clocking the décor, the staring locals, and the potential for a killer cold open. Jolene is immediately game for the attention, while Deputy Bea watches with the expression of someone preparing to regret every second of this.
beats.player_goal: Win the room and start recording without losing control of the conversation.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Della open the interview with a roomful of locals who already think she's a spectacle?
branch_outcomes: Lead with charm and flattery, making Jolene more eager to gossip.
Lead with a bold spooky hook, making Bea openly skeptical right away.
Play it low-key and observational, giving Milo more room to steer toward facts.
beats.exit_state: Della has everyone's attention, but the room is split between amusement, curiosity, and suspicion.

beats.purpose: Deliver the first wave of local lore through Jolene's comic, lurid perspective while burying usable clues inside the gossip.
beats.summary: Jolene pours coffee and gleefully recounts the Sunset Children as the kind of religious weirdos who said grace before fooling around and made the night staff miserable with their midnight singing. Under her embellishments, Della gets a concrete detail: the group stayed in Room 8.
beats.revelation: Jolene says the Sunset Children rented Room 8 and were known for strange hymns sung after midnight.
beats.player_goal: Keep Jolene talking long enough to extract real details from the entertaining nonsense.
beats.choice_prompt: Which part of Jolene's story does Della press for the mic?
branch_outcomes: Push the scandalous angle, getting bigger reactions but messier facts.
Push the practical details, getting clearer information about Room 8 and the group's habits.
Push the fear angle, prompting Jolene to admit the singing genuinely unsettled people.
beats.exit_state: Della has her first solid lead, but it's wrapped in enough performance that Milo can't yet tell how much to trust.

beats.purpose: Introduce Bea as a grounded counterweight who resists Della's sensationalism while unexpectedly validating key parts of the story.
beats.summary: Bea cuts in with dry irritation, reminding Della that missing people are not roadside entertainment and dismissing half of Jolene's flourishes as town folklore with lipstick on it. Still, her corrections confirm the important part: Room 8 appears in the old disappearance record, and no official explanation ever accounted for where the group went.
beats.revelation: Bea confirms that Room 8 is part of the actual case record and that the disappearance was never resolved.
beats.player_goal: Get Bea to keep talking instead of shutting the interview down.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Della respond when Bea accuses her of turning tragedy into content?
branch_outcomes: Trade barbs, escalating friction but keeping Bea engaged.
Pivot to sincerity, framing the podcast as a real investigation and loosening Bea slightly.
Let Milo ask the next question, cooling the room and shifting toward facts.
beats.exit_state: The case gains credibility, but Bea is now actively judging Della's motives.

beats.purpose: Use Milo's fact-finding to align the two conflicting witnesses and uncover the chapel lead.
beats.summary: While Della and Bea spar, Milo quietly asks the follow-up neither performer nor skeptic was prioritizing: where the cult kept trying to go. Both women, for different reasons, point to the same answer—a collapsed chapel outside town that the Sunset Children asked about more than once.
beats.revelation: Jolene and Bea both connect the cult to a ruined chapel outside town, turning rumor into an actionable second lead.
beats.player_goal: Pin down a destination tied to the cult rather than just another colorful story.
beats.exit_state: Della now has two corroborated leads: Room 8 at the motel and the collapsed chapel beyond town.

beats.purpose: Clarify the scene's thematic texture by showing how each local packages horror differently, and let Della decide what kind of story she thinks she has.
beats.summary: As the interview winds through jokes, warnings, and corrections, Della realizes the town has metabolized the disappearance into diner banter, police nuisance, and local branding all at once. Milo points out that the overlap matters more than the contradictions, because every version still circles back to the same room and the same chapel.
beats.revelation: The town's contradictory attitudes are not noise to discard; they are part of how the case has survived and been distorted for thirty years.
beats.player_goal: Decide what to prioritize as the real story hidden underneath the local theater.
beats.choice_prompt: What does Della flag as the most important takeaway from the diner interview?
branch_outcomes: Prioritize the juicy local color for the episode, pleasing Della's brand instincts.
Prioritize Room 8 as the next immediate stop, tightening the investigation.
Prioritize the chapel as the deeper mystery, planting suspicion that the motel is only part of the story.
beats.exit_state: Della stops treating the diner material as mere atmosphere and starts seeing a pattern worth chasing.

beats.purpose: Close the scene with a clean handoff into the next investigation step and lingering character friction.
beats.summary: Bea gives Della a final warning about trespassing, livestreaming, and making extra work for the county, while Jolene cheerfully sends her off with directions, pie, and one last indecent joke about holy rollers. Della leaves energized rather than chastened, convinced that Room 8 is the fastest way to turn diner talk into evidence.
beats.mood: buoyant with a prickle of dread
beats.revelation: Room 8 is the immediate next move, with the chapel marked as the larger mystery waiting beyond it.
beats.player_goal: Leave with momentum, usable audio, and a clear next destination.
beats.exit_state: Della exits the diner with corroborated leads, a wary deputy, a helpful gossip, and renewed confidence that the Sunset Children story may be more than a viral ghost-town anecdote.

color_scheme.reasoning: I chose a palette that blends desert heat, motel neon, and occult unease. The amber-orange accent evokes the Stargazer sign, sunset cult imagery, and podcast-brand energy, while the warm off-whites keep text readable against the game’s likely dark, dusty backgrounds. Hover and selected colors add a brighter neon/survival tension without becoming playful. Muted browns and grays support the grimy motel, dust storm, and subterranean tones, and the overall scheme stays legible while matching the story’s mix of bawdy noir, rural decay, and supernatural dread.
